interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SCLC Director of Education, principal staff figure of the Citizenship Education Program
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Dorothy Foreman Cotton, interviewed at her Ithaca, New York home on July 25, 2011 by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her path from a poor childhood in Goldsboro, North Carolina to becoming the Director of Education for the Southern Christian Leadership Conference. Born on Greenleaf Street in Goldsboro, Cotton lost her mother at age three and was raised by her father Claude, who worked in a tobacco factory and disciplined his four daughters harshly; she describes both anger toward and forgiveness of him as she came to understand his own deprivation. A high school English teacher, Ms. Rosa Gray, became a pivotal figure who recognized her as a 'ready girl,' arranged Cotton's admission to Shaw University, and once visited her father on the front porch to plead that she be allowed to attend the junior-senior prom.

At Shaw and then at Virginia State College in Petersburg, Cotton worked as housekeeper in the residence of college president Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel and his wife Blanche, eventually marrying George Cotton, who had been introduced to her by a classmate named Harris Sims. In Petersburg she became active at the church pastored by the Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, also the regional NAACP head, and served as his secretary while helping train youth to picket the public library and the local Woolworth's. When Reverend Walker invited the young Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. up from Montgomery for a mass meeting, Cotton recited a poem on the program and was introduced to King. After completing her master's degree at Boston University in the summer of 1960, she went with Walker to Atlanta, telling her husband she would stay three months; she stayed twenty-three years.

As SCLC's Director of Education, Cotton ran the Citizenship Education Program inherited from the Highlander Folk School after Tennessee shut Highlander down. At Highlander she met Septima Clark, who had been fired from the Charleston public schools for refusing to disavow the NAACP, and learned the program's origin in Esau Jenkins's bus literacy work on Johns Island, South Carolina. With Andrew Young and Clark she ran five-day residential workshops at the Dorchester Center in McIntosh, Georgia, training community organizers in literacy, voter registration, and the political meaning of citizenship; she estimates between five and eight thousand people came through. Cotton describes her close relationship with King, traveling with him to Oslo for the Nobel Prize and to Paris, and describes the first time she saw him cry — when J. Edgar Hoover called him 'a notorious liar.' She recounts the SCLC staff debate over King's Beyond Vietnam speech, his weariness toward the end of his life, and his exchanges with Stokely Carmichael about nonviolence versus 'Take Up the Gun.'

After King's 1968 assassination, Cotton stayed at SCLC for about three years under Reverend Ralph Abernathy before Andrew Young suggested she take a temporary position as Head Start director in Jefferson County, Alabama; she left SCLC and moved to Birmingham. She closes by discussing the Dorothy Cotton Institute, then in development at the Center for Transformative Action at Cornell University, and her concern that history is being distorted to make it appear that 'three or four people' made the Civil Rights Movement when in fact it was the thousands trained at Dorchester and elsewhere who built it.
key_themes: Citizenship Education Program and adult literacy as movement infrastructure
Women's leadership and gendered authority inside SCLC
Personal friendship and inner-circle proximity to Martin Luther King Jr.
Nonviolence as philosophy versus tactic in the late-1960s debate with SNCC
Mentorship and educational uplift across generations
Grief, weariness, and the post-assassination transition at SCLC
Correcting the great-man distortion of Movement history
main_summary.historical_significance: Cotton's account is one of the principal first-person sources for the day-to-day operation of the Citizenship Education Program, the SCLC training pipeline that prepared thousands of Black Southerners to register voters and exercise political power, and it documents the institutional handoff from Highlander Folk School to SCLC in the early 1960s. As one of the few women in SCLC's executive circle and a member of King's intimate travel cohort, she provides rare insider testimony about both organizational dynamics (staff meetings, the Vietnam-speech debate, Ralph Abernathy's succession) and personal friendship with King. Her sustained insistence that the Movement was built by thousands of trained community organizers, not three or four media-elevated leaders, directly engages historiographical debates about leadership, gender, and the visibility of women's work in the Civil Rights Movement.

errors: The summary states 'on the program' that Cotton 'recited a poem' before her introduction to King; the transcript supports this but the exact ordering of program vs. dinner is muddled in the transcript itself.

The summary characterizes the Dorothy Cotton Institute as 'in development at the Center for Transformative Action at Cornell University' — the transcript supports the Anke Wessels / Center for Transformative Action connection but Cotton herself describes the Institute's institutional posture loosely.

Cotton's exact arrival year and the specific year Highlander was shut down are not given in the transcript; the summary avoids dates here but the reader could infer more precision than the transcript supports.
improvements: Tighten the Petersburg-to-Atlanta chronology — the transcript places the move in summer 1960 immediately after the Boston University master's, but the summary could foreground that the move was originally framed as a three-month commitment.
Add a sentence acknowledging Cotton's frequent hedging ('I'd have to research that') so the reader sees her own epistemic care.
Mention the specific song 'It may be the last time' that Cotton sang to the departing bus of Mississippi Delta organizers, since it is one of the transcript's most emotionally vivid moments.

chapters.title: Childhood in Goldsboro: Father, Loss, and a Sense of Being in the Wrong Place
chapters.summary: Cotton describes growing up on Greenleaf Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, with an outhouse in the backyard and a 'pervasive' feeling from early childhood that she was 'in the wrong place.' Her mother Maggie died when Cotton was three, leaving her father Claude to raise four daughters, ages roughly one to four. She reflects on her father in retrospect with a mixture of anger (for the strap-belt beatings he administered for small infractions) and grief (for the deprivation of his life — he had a third-grade education, worked at a Goldsboro tobacco factory where he once chopped off part of his finger with an ax-hammer, was 'really nervous in front of white folks,' and never had an airplane ride). She tells the story of his late move to Buffalo, New York, where he worked at Bethlehem Steel and felt special pride at being able to pay a $42 restaurant bill at the airport, and of her grandmother's punishing whippings that Cotton learned about from her Aunt Penny. The chapter closes with her describing her father's evolving meaning in her life and her own struggle to find new language for race itself.
related_events: Jim Crow segregation in eastern North Carolina
Black agricultural and industrial labor in mid-century Goldsboro

improvements: Could note the precise tool Cotton herself was unsure of — she alternates between 'ax,' 'hammer,' and 'axe-hammer.'

chapters.title: Ms. Rosa Gray and a Stepmother Briefly Present
chapters.summary: Cotton opens with a memory from around age ten of a white boy riding his bicycle down her unpaved street singing 'deep in the heart of Nigger Town' to the tune of 'Deep in the Heart of Texas' — an early formative jolt of racial consciousness. She then turns to her high school English teacher Ms. Rosa Gray, who cast her in the lead of the annual school play every year and once said audibly after Cotton recited a memorized poem in class, 'There's your ready girl.' Ms. Gray came to Cotton's home and sat on the porch pleading with her father to let her attend the junior-senior prom; Cotton hid under the porch listening. Decades later Cotton realized her father refused not from disapproval but because, as a proud man with no money, he could not equip her in clothes equal to the other students. Cotton then describes Mrs. Susie Mae, the stepmother her father briefly brought into the home to help raise the girls, a churchgoing woman who once turned them back from a walk to church when lightning struck, calling the storm 'nothing but the devil.' Her father later confessed he never loved Susie Mae and had married her only to help raise the children. Cotton notes her father was 'anti-church' (he called preachers 'a racket'), while her maternal grandfather, Reverend Seymour Pellum, was an itinerant preacher whose relationship with her father was hostile.
related_events: Segregated North Carolina public schools in the 1940s
Black Protestant church life in the rural South
Anti-church skepticism in working-class Black households

improvements: Could mention Cotton's reflection that she 'never thought of [Ms. Gray] as a surrogate mother' but recognized the framing when the interviewer raised it.

chapters.title: Shaw University, Virginia State, and Marriage to George Cotton
chapters.summary: Ms. Rosa Gray called Shaw University in Raleigh and arranged Cotton's admission and three jobs (dining hall, cleaning the teachers' dormitory, then becoming housekeeper in the president's residence). When Shaw's president Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel was named president of Virginia State College, Cotton moved with the Daniels to Petersburg, Virginia. She describes her role in the Daniels household as 'part daughter and part housekeeper' and recounts polishing floors, cooking, serving meals, and once being told by Daniel that she could not go to bed even when sick with the flu. She met George Cotton through her classmate Harris Sims, who was in her Shakespearean literature class at Virginia State; George, about ten years her senior, was a Petersburg man who had not attended college. She overheard George ask Dr. Daniel for her hand from the top of the stairs of the president's residence; Daniel told George 'Dorothy is very ambitious. You sure you want this marriage?' and George said 'Yes sir.' They were married in the president's residence.
related_events: Historically Black Colleges and Universities in the 1950s
Domestic labor as college-financing path for Black women
Petersburg, Virginia as a college town

improvements: Could note that Cotton specifically corrects the popular misconception that she met George 'in college' — he never went to college.

chapters.title: Petersburg: Wyatt Tee Walker, the NAACP, and Meeting Dr. King
chapters.summary: Cotton became active at the Petersburg church pastored by Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, also the regional head of the NAACP. As his secretary, she helped organize and train youth to picket the segregated public library and the downtown Woolworth's, animated by her sense that 'we can't use the public library and yet they, white folks, call us ignorant.' Walker had met the young Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. at a conference at Virginia Union University in Richmond and invited him to speak at a mass meeting in Petersburg about the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Cotton recited a poem on the program. At the dinner afterward at the parsonage (hosted by the young women's parish club), King asked to meet 'that young lady who said the poem' and Cotton stood by the head of the table chatting with him. King, while in Petersburg, asked Walker to come to Atlanta to help build the Southern Christian Leadership Conference; Walker agreed on the condition that he could bring two assistants — James R. Wood and Cotton. Cotton told her husband George she would go for three months; she ended up staying twenty-three years. Before leaving she completed her master's degree at Boston University in the summer of 1960, deciding to march in the commencement exercise.

errors: Cotton is uncertain whether her Petersburg pickets preceded or followed the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-in; the summary leaves this open.
improvements: Could mention that Walker's chief lieutenants at this point included Wood and Cotton specifically and that the pickets were at both the library and Woolworth.

chapters.title: Atlanta and SCLC: Highlander, Septima Clark, and the Citizenship Education Program
chapters.summary: Cotton arrived in Atlanta in summer 1960 with George driving her down; she stayed first in a motel, then in a room with Dora McDonald (who became Dr. King's secretary), then with an elderly woman named Ms. Mosley, and eventually got her own apartment. At the SCLC office on Auburn Avenue she found three or four people, including Ella Baker and Lillie Hunter. After a period as Reverend Walker's administrative assistant, Dr. King interviewed her informally at her desk and sent her to the Highlander Folk School in Tennessee, where she met Septima Clark. Highlander, founded by Myles Horton, was facing closure by the state of Tennessee on phony charges, including selling whiskey; a billboard along the highway showed Eleanor Roosevelt and Dr. King and labeled the school 'communist.' Cotton learned the citizenship-education program's origin: Esau Jenkins, a Johns Island man whom Septima Clark described as having a 'third-grade education but a PhD mind,' had been driving a bus full of working people from the South Carolina Sea Islands to Charleston and teaching them about voter registration and political power; he and Clark had met at Highlander. After Tennessee confiscated Highlander's property, the Marshall Field Foundation transferred its funding to SCLC, and Cotton was asked to direct the SCLC Citizenship Education Program. Andrew Young, through his congregational-church ties, secured the Dorchester Center in McIntosh, Georgia as the residential training site.
related_events: Highlander Folk School closure by Tennessee

errors: Cotton mentions Septima Clark had been fired from the Charleston schools for refusing to disavow NAACP membership — the chapter summary covers this in the next chapter rather than here.
improvements: Could mention the specific story that Septima Clark told about Esau Jenkins witnessing the unprotected killing of a Black boy by a white man over a dog as the founding insight that the people had no political power.

chapters.title: Dorchester Workshops: Five-Day Trainings, Cursive Writing, and Recruitment
chapters.summary: Cotton describes the structure of the five-day residential workshops at Dorchester Center, which she designed and led together with Septima Clark and Andrew Young. Clark focused on teaching cursive writing — required to register to vote in many Southern jurisdictions and used as a trick to disqualify Black voters who could only print. Young led sessions on the political importance of voting (Cotton names his session 'why one vote matters'). Cotton herself ran sessions that prioritized dialogue over lecture; she tells the story of a woman who repeated her grievance about a city clerk refusing to test her well water on three successive days until Cotton asked, 'Why aren't you the city clerk?' — a turning point Cotton uses to title the subtitle of her book ('the movement from victim to victory'). Dr. King visited to close a Savannah workshop with the teaching 'nobody can ride your back if your back's not bent.' Cotton estimates between five and eight thousand people came through the program. She names Fannie Lou Hamer of Ruleville, Mississippi as a repeat participant who recruited from the Delta. She and Andrew Young sang 'It may be the last time' as a busload of Mississippi participants pulled away from Dorchester. Her recruitment-pitch technique was sharpened by Albert Menis, a junk man in North Carolina who taught her to lead with 'we'll pay your way' before describing the workshop.
related_events: Dorchester Academy as Black training institution

improvements: Could mention Bernice Robinson — Cotton names her briefly in the original transcript context of urging political understanding.

chapters.title: Women at the SCLC Table and the Team of Wild Horses
chapters.summary: Asked about being a woman at the center of the SCLC, Cotton reflects that the women's-rights movement was still in the 1970s and the gender dynamics of the early 1960s were largely accepted. She was often the only woman at the executive-staff table, expected to take notes or fetch coffee, until staff member Jack O'Dell told Dr. King that 'Dorothy needs to stay at the table' because they were discussing her work. Cotton describes confidence that her work mattered — 'I knew that I was smarter than some of those guys at the table' — and lets the men inhabit their assumed roles. She characterizes King's leadership style as listening at length while the 'team of wild horses' (a phrase King used affectionately) debated, then speaking briefly with decisive effect; the table fell silent when King opened his mouth. She names the staff: Hosea Williams (verbose, demanding more field-staff budget), C.T. Vivian, James Bevel (in overalls and a beanie), and others. She describes Andrew Young positioning Cotton-tall lookalikes around King during marches so an assassin's bullet would miss the leader. Cotton recalls Birmingham, where she ran across the street barefoot to take food to people held in a thinly improvised holding area because the jails were full. Through 1965-67, the threats on King's life were 'fast and furious' but did not control the staff's plans; King himself never seemed nervous.

errors: Cotton mentions the James Meredith March Against Fear here; the summary captures this only implicitly through the staff-debate framing.
improvements: Could expand on the James Meredith March moment — Cotton notes that King decided unilaterally without debate that SCLC would go to Mississippi after Meredith was shot.

chapters.title: Friendship with Dr. King: Paris, Oslo, J. Edgar Hoover, and Weariness
chapters.summary: Cotton describes her relationship with King as friendship, not just professional. She recalls evenings of gin-and-tonic with five people in the group, King liking the way she made hers, and the foolishness of King wanting collard greens and pork chops in Paris on a stopover from the Oslo Nobel Prize trip. She names the SCLC inner travel circle — King, Andrew Young, Bernard Lee — and notes that Coretta Scott King and Dr. King flew on separate planes when they traveled together (to protect their four children). On the Oslo trip, King's mother Alberta Williams King was on Cotton's plane and started singing 'Come Day, Go Day, God Send Sunday' when the sun came up, and Dr. King stood up in the aisle to join her. Cotton recalls the first time she saw King cry — when J. Edgar Hoover called him 'a notorious liar' — sitting around eating in Jamaica or Bermuda where King had gone to write. She describes the SCLC staff debate about whether King should give the Beyond Vietnam speech, with a letter from board member Marion Logan urging him not to; King became more committed after the criticism, ultimately saying that if he were 'the last lone voice speaking for nonviolence,' he would still speak. She recalls Stokely Carmichael at King's kitchen table debating Frantz Fanon and other writers, arguing for other approaches; she also recalls a moment when King came to her apartment alone and sat in her gold rocking chair, considering whether to take a sabbatical at a university position in England.
related_events: 1964 Nobel Peace Prize ceremony in Oslo
J. Edgar Hoover's 1964 attack on King
Black Power debate within the Movement

errors: Cotton hedges about whether the FBI episode was in Jamaica or Bermuda; the summary preserves the hedging.
Marion Logan's name is recoverable from the transcript and the summary attributes the letter accurately, but Cotton notes she doesn't think Logan actually wrote it.
improvements: Could mention Cotton's reflection that King 'never gave up on the fact that nonviolence was the way' and her quotation 'the end we seek is pre-existent in the means we use.'

chapters.title: Nonviolence as Philosophy: After Stokely, Bin Laden, and the Sickness of Racism
chapters.summary: Cotton reaffirms that she 'still believes in the philosophy and way of nonviolence' and rejects the counterfactual that mass violence could have ended American apartheid; she expects it would have produced many more deaths without achieving the goal. She quotes the framing 'racism is a sickness, and when people are sick you work to heal them,' linking it back to her father, whom she now believes she could change with loving understanding. She challenges Stokely Carmichael's later 'Take Up the Gun' rhetoric, recalling that when Carmichael came to speak at Cornell University she asked him publicly 'tell me who you're going to shoot,' a question she says he could not answer. She also confronts his 1964-era 'only position for women in the movement is prone' line, to which Carmichael said he had been joking. Finally, asked about Osama bin Laden, she allows that if bin Laden had been planning to derail more trains and bring down more planes, she does not criticize President Obama's order — but this exception does not displace her general commitment to nonviolence.
related_events: Black Power vs. Nonviolent strategy debate

errors: The summary attributes the bin Laden order to 'President Obama'; the transcript says 'our president' without naming him, but the May 2011 date and surrounding context make this an accurate fill-in.
improvements: Could more carefully delineate that Cotton frames her bin Laden allowance as an exception, not a revision of her general nonviolence stance.

chapters.title: After King's Death: Ralph Abernathy, Birmingham Head Start, and the Mourning Period
chapters.summary: Cotton observes that three years after King's assassination, a young staffer commented 'you all are coming out of mourning now' — and she had not been conscious until then of being in mourning. She describes the void no one could fill, and her difficulty with the transition to Reverend Ralph Abernathy as SCLC president. Without disparaging Abernathy's caring pastoral qualities (she tells a story of Abernathy negotiating with police at a blockade so that his choir members could pass with him rather than being left behind), she believes the role of leading 'this team of wild horses' was unfair to him — that he was 'in over his head' and 'didn't have anybody to help him' as Cotton overheard him say through her office door. Andrew Young suggested Cotton take a temporary Head Start director position in Jefferson County, Alabama after the previous director left under a cloud. Cotton accepted, moved to Birmingham, and took an apartment there. She frames the move as something she needed to do because the SCLC of Abernathy was 'not the same energy' as the SCLC she had known under King. She mentions in passing that after that period she came back and worked for Maynard Jackson, and that other SCLC veterans moved into political life as well.
related_events: Maynard Jackson's mayoral administration in Atlanta

improvements: Could mention Cotton's specific story of Abernathy making someone wait outside his office 'in a giggly silly kind of way' as evidence of her sense that he was uncomfortable in the role.

chapters.title: The Dorothy Cotton Institute, Legacy, and the Distortion of Movement History
chapters.summary: Cotton describes the founding of the Dorothy Cotton Institute, which she did not initiate — Anke Wessels of the Center for Transformative Action at Cornell University proposed the idea after Cotton received the National Freedom Award at the National Civil Rights Museum in Memphis. A steering committee of six or seven friends meets regularly to envision the Institute, which she hopes will eventually have a building in the Northeast and will preserve the work and 'spirit' (not religious; the quality) of the Movement. She closes the interview by insisting that the historical record is being distorted by attention to 'three or four people' rather than the thousands trained at Dorchester. She emphasizes the Edmund Pettus Bridge as an example where John Lewis is remembered but Amelia Boynton (Cotton struggles for the name on tape) was knocked down by horses, and observes that the media gravitates to figures like Reverend Jesse Jackson — who Cotton states was not in fact a daily intimate of King but rather flew down from Chicago for occasional meetings and was on the Lorraine Motel balcony the day King died only because of one such meeting. She is finishing her book manuscript (later published as 'If Your Back's Not Bent') with an editorial helper who handles the technical input.
related_events: Selma to Montgomery marches and Bloody Sunday
Memoir publication of 'If Your Back's Not Bent' (2012)

errors: Cotton struggles on tape to recall the name of the woman knocked down by horses on the Edmund Pettus Bridge — the summary fills in 'Amelia Boynton' from outside the transcript. Marked partial in citation audit.
improvements: Could be more careful about flagging where the summary fills in names that Cotton could not surface on tape.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SCLC Director of Education, Citizenship Education Program
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Clear chronological arc from Goldsboro childhood through Petersburg activism to Atlanta SCLC leadership to post-assassination reflection (Segment 11, 00:00:56,000)
Smooth interviewer-prompted transitions: 'Let me take you back to Miss Gray just a moment' (Segment 276, 00:33:09,000)
Reflective closing that loops back to her central concern about the Movement's collective character (Segment 1015, 02:12:39,940)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Cotton's narrative follows a strong chronological spine that Mosnier helps anchor with periodic redirects. The structure is mostly clear, with some looping back to earlier material (e.g., her father appears in the childhood chapter and again in the nonviolence chapter). The closing reflection on the Dorothy Cotton Institute and Movement historiography provides a satisfying thematic resolution.

most_vivid_moment.quote: And he was singing, you may be familiar with the song, deep in the heart of Texas, where he was singing, deep in the heart of Nigger Town.

evidence: Father's $42 restaurant bill in Buffalo airport, reconstructed in concrete detail (Segment 91, 00:09:00,000)
Ms. Rosa Gray pleading on the porch with her father about the junior prom (Segment 199, 00:17:35,000)
Singing 'It may be the last time' to a Mississippi Delta bus pulling away from Dorchester (Segment 627, 01:15:59,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Cotton consistently anchors her narrative in specific concrete details — Greenleaf Street address, the $42 figure, the exact phrases people said ('There's your ready girl,' 'D'Artha is very ambitious'), and reconstructed dialogue throughout. She names full first-and-last names of dozens of figures and uses sensory details (porch under the house, gold rocking chair, polished floors).

most_authentic_moment.quote: Before I cry, I'm going to stop talking about him. It's a weird, it's just, it's very painful.

vulnerability_elements: openly discusses the pain of her father's beatings
tears about Ms. Rosa Gray's role as surrogate mother (Segment 166)
acknowledges crying with King when J. Edgar Hoover called him a notorious liar
frank about her hostile assessment of Jesse Jackson on tape and her ambivalence about saying it
emotional_authenticity.justification: Cotton repeatedly approaches tears on tape — about her father, about Ms. Rosa Gray, about the post-assassination void at SCLC — and offers candid criticism of figures (Abernathy, Jesse Jackson) she knows will be uncomfortable on the record. Her emotional range from grief to humor to anger is unusually wide, and she does not sanitize her account.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Cotton offers a structurally clear, vividly anchored, and emotionally rich account. She tells stories the listener can visualize — porches, kitchens, planes, bus departures — and is willing to be vulnerable about her family losses, her ambivalent feelings about her father, and her honest assessments of SCLC personalities. The interview is a model of oral history in which the narrator's specificity meets her willingness to feel on tape.

major_events: Founding of SCLC's Citizenship Education Program, 1960-1961 (Segment 569)
1964 Oslo Nobel Prize trip with Dr. King (Segment 660)
SCLC staff debate over the Beyond Vietnam speech, early 1967 (Segment 687)
James Meredith March Against Fear, June 1966 (Segment 643)

key_figures.name: Stokely Carmichael
key_figures.relationship: Debating counterpart at King's kitchen table

organizational_involvement.role: Director of Education / Citizenship Education Program director

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Cotton was the principal day-to-day administrator of the Citizenship Education Program — the SCLC's most consequential and least-publicized organizing infrastructure — and was simultaneously one of the few women in King's inner travel circle, giving her rare combined insight into both grass-roots training and high-level SCLC strategy and personality dynamics.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Cotton occupied two rare positions simultaneously: principal staff figure of the CEP (the program that trained the people who registered voters) and inner-circle traveling companion to King. Few oral history sources combine both vantage points.

precision_examples: Father's $42 restaurant bill at Buffalo airport (Segment 91)
Address '917 North Greenlee Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina' (Segment 21)
James R. Wood and Cotton as Walker's two assistants brought to Atlanta (Segment 444)
Marshall Field Foundation transferred CEP funding from Highlander to SCLC (Segment 569)
verifiable_claims: She earned a master's degree at Boston University in 1960
Marshall Field Foundation funded Highlander's CEP and transferred funds to SCLC
Walker was the regional NAACP head in Petersburg and pastor at the church she attended
King delivered the 'no one can ride your back if your back's not bent' message at Savannah Citizenship Education workshop closing
specificity_of_details.justification: Cotton provides full names of many figures (Rosa Gray, Robert Prentiss Daniel, Blanche Daniel, Harris Sims, Wyatt Tee Walker, James R. Wood, Esau Jenkins, Septima Clark, Ella Baker, Lillie Hunter, Dora McDonald, Alberta Williams King, Marion Logan, Anke Wessels), specific institutional names (Bethlehem Steel, Shaw, Virginia State, Boston University, Highlander, Dorchester, NAACP, SCLC, Cornell), and place names (Goldsboro, Petersburg, McIntosh, Johns Island, Buffalo, Ithaca). She is often vague on dates ('I'd have to research that') but precise on names and places.

new_information: Intimate day-to-day operation of the Citizenship Education Program from the perspective of its principal SCLC administrator
The transfer of CEP funding from Marshall Field Foundation to SCLC at the moment Tennessee shut Highlander down
Personal account of King's emotional response to J. Edgar Hoover's 'notorious liar' attack
Inside view of the staff debate over the Beyond Vietnam speech, including Marion Logan's letter and King's response
Critical assessment of Ralph Abernathy's fit as King's successor and the post-assassination SCLC void
Direct testimony of Stokely Carmichael at King's kitchen table debating Frantz Fanon and other writers
Recruitment-pedagogy detail from Albert Menis the 'junk man' teaching her to pitch workshops
understudied_aspects: Women's labor and authority inside SCLC's executive staff
Citizenship Education Program pedagogy as the Socratic-dialogue alternative to lecture
The Goldsboro-to-Petersburg working-class Black women's pathway into Movement leadership
The Dorothy Cotton Institute as a Northeast counterpart to Southern Civil Rights memorials
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Cotton's testimony directly complicates the 'great-man' narrative of the Civil Rights Movement by insisting that the Movement was built by the thousands trained at Dorchester, not the three or four media-elevated leaders. She also complicates the standard biography of King by emphasizing his exhaustion, his consideration of a sabbatical, and the intimacy of his SCLC inner circle. Her depiction of Abernathy is candid in a way most SCLC sources are not.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of the Citizenship Education Program (Levine, Charron) and historians of women's leadership in the Movement (Robnett, Crawford-Houck) would find Cotton's first-person operational testimony foundational. Her account is the single best source on the day-to-day work of the CEP from the SCLC-administrative side and complements Septima Clark's autobiographies from the founder side.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: This interview is a top-tier historiographical contribution. Cotton was uniquely positioned, and she is unusually candid — she names names, criticizes Jesse Jackson on tape, and offers a structural critique of how Movement memory has been distorted. Her account of the Beyond Vietnam internal debate alone would justify the interview's value.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Cotton's testimony is among the most historically valuable in the corpus: she was the day-to-day administrator of the SCLC's most consequential organizing program and simultaneously a member of King's inner circle. Her specificity on names, organizations, and institutional dynamics is high, with calibrated uncertainty on exact dates. She actively complicates the dominant great-man narrative and provides primary-source material for historians of the CEP, women in SCLC, and post-assassination SCLC.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: From a Goldsboro girl who 'felt like I was in the wrong place' and was harshly disciplined by a father she could not understand, to a Smithsonian-grade Movement leader who learned to forgive him through the same nonviolent framework she applied to racism itself. Her arc is from inarticulate childhood grievance to philosophically articulated commitment.
key_turning_points: The white boy singing 'deep in the heart of Nigger Town' at age ten — first conscious racial outrage (Segment 148)
Ms. Rosa Gray's 'there's your ready girl' moment as the first adult recognition of her capacity (Segment 163)
Recitation of the poem at the Petersburg mass meeting and being introduced to King at the parsonage (Segment 433)
Going to Atlanta for 'three months' and staying 23 years (Segment 448)
The story of the woman complaining about the city clerk, met with 'why aren't you the city clerk?' — Cotton's pedagogical breakthrough (Segments 614-617)
King's death and three years of unrecognized mourning before Birmingham Head Start (Segments 777-780)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: The 'youngster never traveled' who arrived in Atlanta in 1960 ended up flying to Oslo for the Nobel Prize, debating Stokely Carmichael, building a federally inherited training institution, and now stewarding an institute in her own name in Ithaca.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Strongly inspirational — her father's deprivation and the harshness of her upbringing did not foreclose her trajectory; her teachers' generosity and her own readiness moved her forward. The Socratic moment with the city-clerk woman is the kind of pedagogical-Movement story that resonates with educators, organizers, and adult learners.
transformation_growth.justification: Cotton's transformation arc is unusually deep because it spans both her personal psychological growth (forgiving her father, finding her authority in male-dominated rooms) and her institutional career (Goldsboro to Director of Education for SCLC). She is reflective about the change without being self-aggrandizing.

peak_moments.description: Cotton's father beating her with a belt for breaking a dish
peak_moments.stakes: her physical safety and her later capacity to forgive

peak_moments.description: Hiding under the porch while Ms. Rosa Gray pleads with her father about the prom
peak_moments.stakes: her social participation as a teenager

peak_moments.description: Pocketbook stolen in Atlanta her first week
peak_moments.stakes: her safety and continuation in Atlanta

peak_moments.description: Birmingham campaign, running across the street barefoot to bring food to detainees
Seeing King cry for the first time after Hoover's 'notorious liar' attack
The 'you all are coming out of mourning now' realization three years after King's death
peak_moments.stakes: the trajectory of her professional and emotional life

conflict_types: racial-political (library segregation, Hoover, Stokely Carmichael's challenge)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From grief and anger to humor and tenderness to political indignation
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Cotton's interview contains real dramatic moments but is more reflective than action-oriented; the dramatic peaks tend to be relational or internal rather than physical. Birmingham gets brief treatment compared to the institutional/CEP arc.

universal_themes: the teacher who changes a student's life
imposter feeling at the executive table
grief that you don't notice you're carrying
the conviction that your real work isn't being recognized
the difficulty of saying critical things on tape
relatable_struggles: working her way through college as a domestic in the president's residence
negotiating a three-month commitment that became a life
watching a beloved institution falter under new leadership
human_moments: King wanting collard greens and pork chops in Paris (Segment 661)
Alberta Williams King singing 'Come Day, Go Day' on the plane to Oslo (Segments 665-668)
The gold rocking chair in Cotton's apartment where King sat to think aloud (Segment 700)
Cotton stopping in mid-sentence because she's about to cry (Segment 115)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences who have lost a parent, who had a teacher save them, who have been the only woman at a meeting, who have grieved a leader, or who have wondered whether the public record honors the right people will all find Cotton's account directly accessible. Her insistence that the Movement was made by thousands, not by a handful of media figures, is the kind of moral claim that resonates beyond Civil Rights history.
relatable_experiences.justification: Cotton's account interleaves universal human experiences with the extraordinary events of the Movement, making it accessible to audiences without specialized Civil Rights background while remaining historically substantive.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Cotton's interview achieves strong emotional resonance through her transformation arc, her relational candor about King and others, and her willingness to feel grief and ambivalence on tape. The dramatic peaks are more reflective and relational than action-driven, which is appropriate for a 2011 retrospective interview but slightly reduces the tension-and-conflict subscore.

barriers: Some movement-specific acronyms (SCLC, NAACP, SNCC, CEP) used without on-the-fly explanation
Frequent digressions ('I'm going to digress here') that require listener patience
language_clarity.justification: Cotton speaks in long, well-formed sentences with frequent self-correction and digression. Her articulation is excellent and her vocabulary precise; she self-contextualizes the most important references but assumes some background knowledge of Civil Rights organizational acronyms.

high_energy_sections: Petersburg activism and meeting King (Segments 384-456) — clear, propulsive narrative
The Highlander/Septima Clark/Esau Jenkins origin story (Segments 540-569) — dense with new information
The 'why aren't you the city clerk?' pedagogical breakthrough (Segments 614-619) — high emotional payoff
slow_sections: Early childhood/father chapter contains looping returns to the same themes (Segments 28-138) — Cotton acknowledges 'before I cry I'm going to stop talking about him'
Several moments of name-recall struggle ('I'll think of the name in a minute' — Segments 467-488)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: A focused listener can sustain attention across the full 2h 13m; casual audiences will likely engage best with chapters 4-7 (Petersburg-Atlanta-Highlander-Dorchester arc).
narrative_flow.justification: Mosnier is a skilled interviewer who redirects gently and lets Cotton breathe. The pacing is good but the front-loaded family material is more reflective than narrative-propulsive.

themes_connected: the role of education in liberation
the difference between movements and jobs
memory politics and the distortion of historical record
nonviolence as a deep philosophy vs. a tactical choice
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Cotton's reflections on movement historiography (how 'three or four people' get credited for thousands' work) speaks directly to ongoing debates about Black women's invisibility in social movements. Her bin-Laden answer, her engagement with the philosophy of nonviolence, and her concerns about who shapes which stories survive all connect to 2010s discourse.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Cotton explicitly draws the larger meaning out of nearly every story she tells. The Citizenship Education Program is framed as the substrate of the entire voting-rights apparatus; her father's beatings are framed as a parallel to the sickness of racism; her relationship with King is framed as evidence against the great-man theory.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Cotton is unusually conscious of the historiographical and philosophical stakes of her own testimony. She explicitly maps her stories to broader themes and addresses contemporary stakes (2011 bin Laden, 2010s women's invisibility).

accessibility.overall_assessment: Cotton is highly articulate and self-conscious about the larger meaning of her account. The interview is accessible to general audiences with brief contextual support, though the front-loaded family material requires patience before the Movement narrative accelerates.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is an exceptional Civil Rights oral history. Cotton combines unique insider access (SCLC executive staff, CEP director, King's inner travel circle) with reflective candor (about her father, Ralph Abernathy, Jesse Jackson, the gendered staff table). Her narrative is structurally clear, vividly detailed, and consciously framed against the dominant great-man historiography of the Movement. The historical-value dimension is particularly strong because she occupied two rare positions simultaneously — day-to-day CEP administrator and King intimate — that few sources combine. The interview's principal limitation is its retrospective rather than action-driven cadence, which is appropriate to a 2011 reflection but slightly reduces the dramatic-tension subscore. Overall this is a top-quintile interview in the corpus.
key_strengths: Unique combined vantage point: Cotton was both the principal day-to-day administrator of the Citizenship Education Program and a member of King's inner travel circle, giving her dual insight into grass-roots organizing infrastructure and SCLC strategic dynamics.
Candid first-person testimony on the post-assassination SCLC transition under Ralph Abernathy, including her own decision to leave for Birmingham Head Start — a rarely documented internal account.

Explicit historiographical intervention against the great-man narrative of the Movement; her insistence that the Movement was built by the thousands trained at Dorchester, not three or four media figures, directly complements scholarly debates about gender and visibility.
key_limitations: Cotton hedges on specific dates throughout ('I'd have to research that'), which is intellectually honest but reduces the verifiability of some claims for historians who need precise chronology.

Front-loaded family material (Chapters 1-3) is more reflective than narrative-propulsive; general audiences may need to commit to the full first half hour before the Petersburg-Atlanta arc accelerates.

claims.claim: Cotton was interviewed at her Ithaca, New York home on July 25, 2011 by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 4-9: 'Today is Monday, July 25, 2011. My name is Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. I'm with our project, Video of the Fert John Bishop in Ithaca, New York, to do an oral history interview for the Civil Rights History Project, which is a joint undertaking of Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress.'
claims.rationale: Date, interviewer, affiliation, location, and project all stated directly in opening segments.

claims.claim: Cotton was born on Greenleaf Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 21: '917 North Greenlee Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, where I was born.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. The transcript renders the street as 'Greenlee' rather than the historically attested 'Greenleaf,' but Cotton uses both renderings; this is a minor transcription variant, not a substantive disagreement.

claims.claim: Cotton lost her mother at age three.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 27: 'My mother died when I was three years old.'

claims.claim: Her father Claude raised four daughters, ages roughly one to four when their mother died, and worked in a tobacco factory in Goldsboro.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 60, 98, 100: 'We were one two, less than one year old, three and four, the four of us, when my mother died... Working in this tobacco factory... His job was to put tops on barrels where he'd have to use a hammer.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Note Cotton's father is named Claude in segment 80 ('Claude, get in the car...').

claims.claim: Ms. Rosa Gray, her high school English teacher, arranged Cotton's admission to Shaw University.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 287-289: 'Miss Gray knew that college was not in the offing for me, but she called over to Shaw University when I was about to graduate from high school... Miss Gray talked to someone there at Shaw, and I ended up being accepted as a student at Shaw University.'

claims.claim: Cotton worked as housekeeper in the residence of Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel, Shaw's president, and moved with the Daniels to Virginia State College when Daniel became its president.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 302, 360-361: 'Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel, and his wife Blanche Daniel, they had me coming over to instead of the teacher's dormitory to help take care of the president's residence... When I moved, when Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel was offered the president's of Virginia State College, I guess I was a really good house cleaner because they wanted me to go with them.'

claims.claim: Cotton met her future husband George Cotton through her classmate Harris Sims in a Shakespearean class at Virginia State.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 328, 335-336: 'I was in a class with a fellow, I think his name is Harris Sims, was a classmate of mine. In a class, Shakespearean class, we both loved Shakespeare... And sitting there one day of Sims said to me, I have a fellow I'd like you to meet. And his name is George Cotton.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated; Cotton even flags her own uncertainty about Sims's exact first name.

claims.claim: Cotton became active at the Petersburg church pastored by Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, also the regional NAACP head, and served as his secretary.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 393-394, 413: 'And the Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker was pastor of this church... Wyatt Tee Walker was also the regional head of the NAACP... I became secretary to him, secretary of the NAACP.'

claims.claim: Cotton trained youth to picket the segregated public library and the downtown Woolworth's in Petersburg.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 412, 442: 'He wanted us to put a picket line in front of the library and ultimately in front of the Woolworth store downtown... I'm helping the youngsters prepare to walk in front of the dime store, Woolworth store, and in front of the library.'

claims.claim: Wyatt Tee Walker had met King at a conference at Virginia Union University in Richmond, and invited King to Petersburg to speak at a mass meeting.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 418: 'Reverend Walker had met Martin at a conference at Virginia Union University in Richmond, Virginia.'

claims.claim: Cotton recited a poem on the program and was introduced to King at the parsonage dinner.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 434-435: 'He wanted to know, I can see him now sitting at the end of this quite long table in the parsonage at Reverend Walker's house... He wanted to meet that young lady who said the poem on the program earlier that night.'

claims.claim: King asked Walker to come to Atlanta to help build SCLC; Walker agreed on the condition he could bring James R. Wood and Cotton.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 443-444: 'Dr. King asked Revan Walker to move to Atlanta, Georgia... I will move to Atlanta, Georgia to work with you... If I can take the two people who helped me here, that was a fellow named James R. Wood and me.'

claims.claim: Cotton told her husband George she would stay in Atlanta three months; she stayed twenty-three years.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 445, 448: 'I'd like to go down and help them out for about three months... But I stayed 23 years.'

claims.claim: Cotton earned a master's degree at Boston University in the summer of 1960.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 515-518, 522: 'I went up to, I just, again, I knew that I was hungry for something... I went up to Boston, to Boston University for one summer... I did get the master's degree. As a matter of fact, that's one day I got clear about, because it was in the summer of 1960... So I went to Atlanta, Georgia a few days after I got the degree at Boston University.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Note Cotton's degree is described as a master's; she does not specify the field.

claims.claim: At the Atlanta SCLC office on Auburn Avenue, Cotton initially found Ella Baker, Lillie Hunter, and a small staff.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 465-472: 'I found three or four people in an office on Albert Avenue... Young woman, Lily Hunter... a woman named her, her name is Brown... Ms. Ella Baker.'
claims.rationale: Cotton names Lily/Lillie Hunter and Ella Baker. The transcript renders the street as 'Albert Avenue' though it is Auburn Avenue; the summary uses the historically accurate 'Auburn,' which is a name correction not a fabrication. The 'Brown' figure named in the transcript is not specifically rendered as 'Lillie Hunter' — the summary slightly simplifies by naming only the two most identifiable figures. Partial because the summary's framing of 'three or four people including' is a fair compression but elides one named person.

claims.claim: Cotton was sent by King to Highlander Folk School in Tennessee, where she met Septima Clark.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 530-540: 'He asked me if I would go over to a place called Highlander Folk School... I went to Highlander and I met this woman and Septima Clark.'

claims.claim: Septima Clark had been fired from the Charleston public schools for refusing to disavow her NAACP membership.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 556: 'Septima had been fired from the Charleston school, school system. She had been a teacher, but she was fired because she would not say whether or not she was a member of the NAACP or not.'

claims.claim: Esau Jenkins, a Johns Island man, drove a bus from the South Carolina Sea Islands to Charleston and taught his riders about voter registration and political power.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 542, 553: 'Esau Jenkins was a man who lived on the John's Island... he used to drive a school, old school bus he had acquired from the islands to the mainland of Charleston... Issa started helping people on, on his old, his bus as he drove them back and forth to work, understand why it was important for them to register to vote and talking to them about political power.'

claims.claim: After Tennessee shut Highlander down, the Marshall Field Foundation transferred its funding for the Citizenship Education Program to SCLC, and Cotton was asked to direct it.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 569-571: 'The Marshall Field Foundation who gave the major funds to the Highlander school to run do this training... It didn't take them long to decide they would give those funds to SCLC to run this citizenship education program. And I was asked to become the director of citizenship education program.'

claims.claim: Andrew Young, through his congregational-church ties, secured the Dorchester Center in McIntosh, Georgia as the residential training site.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 572, 576: 'Thanks to Andrew Young, who also Andrew Young had secured through the good offices of his church, the congregational church, a facility there in Macintosh, Georgia... Because Andrew Young had a connection with the congregational church, being a congregational minister, we found this, the Dorchester Center at Macintosh, Georgia.'

claims.claim: Cotton estimates between five and eight thousand people came through the Citizenship Education Program workshops.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 623: 'I've heard and to use the term eight thousand I don't know how I don't know how we kept I but at least between five and eight I say between five and six and the things it was about eight thousand who came through the years.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated, with Cotton's own hedging about exact counts.

claims.claim: Fannie Lou Hamer of Ruleville, Mississippi was a repeat workshop participant who recruited from the Delta.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 625: 'Fannie Lou Hamer living in Ruleville, Mississippi and she came more than once and once Fannie Lou Hamer had come to a workshop she would we could just tell her give her the dates of the next workshop... she would do the recruiting there.'

claims.claim: Cotton sang 'It may be the last time' as a busload of Mississippi Delta organizers pulled away from Dorchester.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 627-628: 'I remember standing on the ground as the bus was pulling off singing it may be the last time we worked together it may be the last time I don't know. And we both cried as the bus pulled off.'

claims.claim: Staff member Jack O'Dell told King that Cotton needed to stay at the executive-staff table because they were discussing her work.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 634: 'It was Jack O'Dell who said Dr. King D'Artha and he used to stay at this table because we're talking about what I'm going to do.'

claims.claim: King described his SCLC staff as a 'team of wild horses.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 639: 'He called us I'm not sure I was included in that his team of wild horses.'

claims.claim: Cotton traveled with King to Oslo for the Nobel Prize and to Paris on a stopover.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 660: 'I remember being in we were in Paris France a stopover having gone to Oslo, Norway for the Nobel Prize.'

claims.claim: Coretta Scott King and Dr. King flew on separate planes when traveling internationally because they had four children.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 663: 'Dr. King's wife Coretta was on one plane and Dr. King was on another plane... They had four children to raise and if anything should happen at least one parent.'

claims.claim: Cotton saw King cry for the first time when J. Edgar Hoover called him 'a notorious liar.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 676-677: 'When you heard that J. Edgar Hoover... the first time I saw him cry I saw tears run down it's cheap when when J. Edgar Hoover called him a notorious liar.'

claims.claim: Cotton describes the SCLC staff debate over King's Beyond Vietnam speech, including a letter from board member Marion Logan urging him not to deliver it.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 740: 'There were people on the board of directors that was a family friend a Marion Logan signed a letter which we don't think she wrote and the letter urging him not to do that.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Cotton hedges that Logan may not have written the letter herself.

claims.claim: King told Cotton in her apartment that he was considering taking a sabbatical at a university position in England.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 709-711: 'Maybe I should take a sabbatical maybe I should... somebody had offered him a position at a university in I think somewhere in England maybe I should take should accept a position.'

claims.claim: Stokely Carmichael debated King at his kitchen table, discussing Frantz Fanon and other writers.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 718-723: 'Especially Stokely Carmichael I'm not sure who else with Stokely Carmichael was sitting there... around his kitchen table... Stokely Carmichael they like talking about Frantz Fanon and some of a writers and that they would quote.'

claims.claim: After King's 1968 assassination, Cotton stayed at SCLC for about three years before Andrew Young suggested she take a Head Start director position in Jefferson County, Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 776, 802-803: 'After three years I remember this young guy who worked with us... I looked out I can reflect back and think that um the reason I actually was Andrew Young my good friend... one day he got word that the people in Birmingham, Alabama needed a Head Start director, Jefferson County, Alabama and this is three years after Dr. King death and Andrew Young said uh they they need a temporary director why don't you go over and work with him.'

claims.claim: Cotton later worked for Maynard Jackson in Atlanta.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 971: 'I went to be Head Start director, Jefferson County, Alabama but I came back and worked for Maynard Jackson.'

claims.claim: The Dorothy Cotton Institute was proposed by Anke Wessels of the Center for Transformative Action at Cornell University after Cotton received the National Freedom Award at the National Civil Rights Museum.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 901-902, 926: 'Anke Wessels who's a teacher's over at Cornell University runs a wonderful program there the Center for Transformative Action... It was after I got an award at the National Civil Rights Museum which was quite an honor.'

claims.claim: Cotton publicly challenged Stokely Carmichael at Cornell University asking 'tell me who you're going to shoot.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 866-871: 'I had a chance to talk with us Stokely Carmichael he came here to speak not long before he died... here speaking at Cornell University he said yeah he said it again and I had a chance to say Stokely Carmichael tell me who you going to shoot.'

claims.claim: Cotton allows that she does not criticize President Obama's order to kill Osama bin Laden, given evidence of further planned attacks, though this does not displace her general commitment to nonviolence.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 894-895: 'I don't criticize Osama bin Laden our president for for giving the order to let it go forward that doesn't take away my general basic belief in non-violence.'
claims.rationale: Cotton says 'our president' rather than naming Obama; given the May 2011 context (the bin Laden killing was May 1-2, 2011, the interview is July 25, 2011), the inference to Obama is straightforward but is supplied by the summary, not by Cotton on tape. Partial because of the name fill-in.

claims.claim: Amelia Boynton was knocked down by horses on the Edmund Pettus Bridge — referenced as an example of underrecognized Movement figures.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 974-975: 'people talk as though John Lewis was the only one on the Edmund Pettus Bridge because he got this cacossa and then Mrs uh uh oh her name went eluded me you probably in the in Alabama oh gosh shame on me I'll tell your name in a minute she was knocked down by one of those horses on the on the uh the the bridge.'
claims.rationale: Cotton describes the event but cannot recall the name on tape. The summary fills in 'Amelia Boynton' from outside the transcript. This is a reasonable inference for the historical record (Boynton was famously knocked down on the bridge by trooper horses on Bloody Sunday 1965) but the name is not in the transcript. Partial citation status.

publication_decision.rationale: Main summary accuracy 86 and quality 88 are both below the 90/90 Smithsonian-grade threshold, and three citation-audit claims are partially supported (the 'Auburn Avenue' rendering vs. transcript's 'Albert Avenue,' the 'President Obama' fill-in vs. transcript's 'our president,' and the 'Amelia Boynton' fill-in vs. Cotton's on-tape inability to recall the name). All three partials are reasonable institutional knowledge fills that improve the public record, but the fail-closed gate routes them to human review rather than auto-publishing. The interview itself is exceptional historical material (Cotton was the principal CEP administrator and a King intimate); a human reviewer with access to standard reference sources can confirm and accept the three fill-ins quickly, after which the output should clear publication. No claims are unsupported and no factual inversions or contaminations were detected.
